content.zh:
Permutation and Puzzles (5)

Yujian Song

In this tutorial, we are going to talk about how to separate a series of disjoint 3-cycles.

A series of disjoint 3-cycles is common when solving puzzles. We have known that if A and B have one common element only, the commutator [A, B] is a 3-cycle; and if there are two common elements, and they are not adjacent in A and B, [A, B] is two disjoint 3-cycles.

And if A and B have multiple common elements, we can still get disjoint 3-cycles as long as these common elements are not adjacent in A and B. The “not adjacent” here means being not adjacent in permutations instead of in puzzles. For example, assuming the common elements affected by A and B as 1,2,3,…,n, for any 1≤i≠j≤n, there are always A(i)≠j and B(i)≠j, then [A, B] is n disjoint 3-cycles:

[A, B] = (1,B'(1),A'(1))(2,B'(2),A'(2))…(n,B'(n),A'(n)).

Here is an example. In 3×3×3 Cube, if we let A=R2 and B=U2, as the image below, there are three common elements affected by R2 and U2, two corner blocks and an edge block. Obviously, these three blocks cannot be replaced with each other, so the commutator [A, B] = R2 U2 R2 U2 is three disjoint 3-cycles:

Or we can change our mind. The relative position of these three blocks does not change after doing [A, B], so we can imagine that if these three blocks are glued together, becoming a big glued block, the operation [A, B] = R2 U2 R2 U2 can still be done, and then we get a 3-cycle of bandaged blocks, which is three disjoint 3-cycles after unbandaging.

In FTO, there are three common blocks affected by rotation F and R’, so we can know that F R' F' R can cause the three disjoint 3-cycles below:

Commonly, constructing a series of disjoint 3-cycles is much easier than finding a simple 3-cycle, because it is hard to find operation A and B having one common element only. Therefore when solving puzzles, it will be much more convenient if we can find a way to separate a specific 3-cycle from a series of disjoint 3-cycles. Here are two ways commonly used:

  1. Construct New Commutators Using Commutators as Elements

Commonly, the disjoint 3-cycles created by commutators are much simpler than the basic rotation permutation structure of the puzzle. Base on this idea, we can try to find two series of disjoint 3-cycles having one common element only.

In FTO, for example, here are two series of disjoint 3-cycles in different directions, which have one common corner block only. So we can use these two commutators to generate a new commutator, to get the 3-cycle formula of corner blocks: [[F, R'], L: [U, Rd']] = F R' F' R L U Rd' U' Rd L' R' F R F' L Rd' U Rd U' L', adding a conjugate on the second commutator in order to ensure these two commutators affecting corner blocks only.

  1. Use Formula of the Form (A B A B')2 to Separate 3-cycles

Make a series of disjoint 3-cycles, assuming . All elements affected by constitute the set , in which every element is different from each other, and all elements affected by constitute the set , meeting the following conditions:

  2. ,

Then .

In other word, if does not affect either to or any and , and can move from to to other side (to avoid being affected by ), the formula can separate the first 3-cycles in .

Just like the examples of 3×3×3 Cube and FTO above, we can regard as a bandaged block , and gain bandaged blocks and in the same way. Then the permutation becomes a 3-cycle of , and is a permutation cutting but keeping and . The effect of formula is to remove the cut-off part from the original 3-cycle.

For example, have a look at these two permutations below:

Using this method, we can get the 3-cycle formula of center blocks in FTO:

We still try to separate [F, R’] = F R’ F’ R, the three disjoint 3-cycles below. It is easy to see that Rd can cut off an imaginary bandaged block, remaining a center block in its original place, and keep the other two bandaged blocks. Therefore the formula ([F, R’] Rd [F, R’] Rd’)2 = (F R' F' R Rd F R' F' R Rd')2 can separate the 3-cycle of center blocks.

It is not different to construct a new commutator using commutators as elements to get the 3-cycle of center blocks, as the first method said. Interested readers can think for yourselves. (Tips: Rotations of middle layers are necessary.)
